coredata.dc:title: Sandwich-type electrochemical aptasensor based on hemin-graphite oxide as a signal label and rGO/MWCNTs/chitosan/carbon quantum dot modified electrode for sensitive detection of Acinetobacter baumannii bacteria
coredata.dc:creator: Abedi, Rokhsareh
Raoof, Jahan Bakhsh
Mohseni, Mojtaba
Bagheri Hashkavayi, Ayemeh
coredata.dc:description: Acinetobacter baumannii (A. baumannii) is a pathogenic bacterium that causes severe infections and its rapid and reliable diagnosis is essential for effective control and treatment. In this study, we present an electrochemical aptasensor based on a signal amplification strategy for the detection of A. baumannii, the high specificity and affinity of the aptamer for the target make it favorable for signal amplification. This allows for a highly sensitive and selective detection of the target. The aptasensor is based on a carbon screen-printed electrode (CSPE) that has been modified with a nanocomposite consisting of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), chitosan (CS), and a synthesized carbon quantum dot (CQD) from CS. Additionally, the self-assembled aptamers were immobilized on hemin-graphite oxide (H-GO) as a signal probe. The composition of the nanocomposite (rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD) provides high conductivity and stability, facilitating the efficient capture of A. baumannii onto the surface of the aptasensor. Also, aptamer immobilized on Hemin-graphite oxide (H-GO/Aptamer) was utilized as an electrochemical signal reporter probe by H reduction. This approach improved the detection sensitivity and the aptamer surface density for detecting A. baumannii. Furthermore, under optimized experimental conditions, the aptasensor was demonstrated to be capable of detecting A. baumannii with a linear range of (10 – 1 × 107 Colony-forming unit (CFU)/mL) and a limit of detection (LOD) of 1 CFU/mL (σ = 3). One of the key features of this aptasensor is its ability to distinguish between live and dead bacteria cells, which is very important and critical for clinical applications. In addition, we have successfully detected A. baumannii bacteria in healthy human serum and skim milk powder samples provided using the prepared electrochemical aptasensor. The functional groups present in the synthetic CQD, rGO-MWCNT, and chitosan facilitate biomolecule immobilization and enhance stability and activity. The fast electron-transfer kinetics and high conductivity of these materials contribute to improved sensitivity and selectivity. Furthermore, The H-GO/Aptamer composite's large surface area increases the number of immobilized secondary aptamers and enables a more stable structure. This large surface area also facilitates more H loading, leading to signal amplification.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FE-SEM images of bare CSPE (A), GO/CSPE (B), rGO/CSPE (C), rGO-MWCNT/CSPE (D) and rGO-MWCNT/CS/CSPE (E), rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/CSPE (F), GO (G), H-GO (H) and CQD (I).

(A) FT-IR spectra of (a) GO and (b) rGO, (B) FT-IR spectra of (a) MWCNT and (b) MWCNT-COOH, (C) FTIR spectra (a) CS and (b) CQD, (D) XRD pattern of (a) GO and (b) rGO, (E) XRD patterns of (a) GO and (b) H-GO and (F) XRD patterns of (a) CS and (b) CQD.

(A) Nyquist plots and (B) cyclic voltammograms of the bare CSPE (a), rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/CSPE (b), Apt/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/CSPE (c), MCE/Apt/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/CSPE (d), A. baumannii/MCE/Apt/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/CSPE (e), H-GO/Aptamer/A. baumannii/MCE/Apt/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/CSPE (f) in 0.01 M [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- containing 0.10 M KCl at 100 mV/s scan rate. The inset in (A) is Randles equivalent circuit for EIS.

DPV signals in the absence of target aptamers and A. baumannii (a), in the presence of target aptamers without A. baumannii (b), in the absence of target aptamers with A. baumannii (c), and in the presence of target aptamers with A. baumannii (d).

(A) DPV curves and (B) corresponding calibration plot of the aptasensor for determination of A. baumannii with various concentrations: (a) 0.00, (b) 10, (c) 1 × 102, (d) × 103, (e) 5 × 103, (f) 1 × 104, (g) 5 × 104, (h) 1 × 105, (i) 5 × 105, (j) 1 × 106, (k) 5 × 106 and (l) 1 × 107 CFU/mL in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4), error bar = SD (n = 3).

(A) DPV signals and (B) histogram of selectivity studies of the proposed electrochemical aptasensor in the presence of some common bacteria for the selective voltammetric detection of A. baumannii in PBS (0.1 M, pH = 7.4), error bar = SD (n = 3).
(A). Synthesis steps of rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD, (B) Synthesis steps of H-GO/Aptamer and (C) schematic illustration of the aptasensor fabrication.

head.ce:title: Sandwich-type electrochemical aptasensor based on hemin-graphite oxide as a signal label and rGO/MWCNTs/chitosan/carbon quantum dot modified electrode for sensitive detection of Acinetobacter baumannii bacteria

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Acinetobacter baumannii (A. baumannii) is a pathogenic bacterium that causes severe infections and its rapid and reliable diagnosis is essential for effective control and treatment. In this study, we present an electrochemical aptasensor based on a signal amplification strategy for the detection of A. baumannii, the high specificity and affinity of the aptamer for the target make it favorable for signal amplification. This allows for a highly sensitive and selective detection of the target. The aptasensor is based on a carbon screen-printed electrode (CSPE) that has been modified with a nanocomposite consisting of multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs), reduced graphene oxide (rGO), chitosan (CS), and a synthesized carbon quantum dot (CQD) from CS. Additionally, the self-assembled aptamers were immobilized on hemin-graphite oxide (H-GO) as a signal probe.

The composition of the nanocomposite (rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD) provides high conductivity and stability, facilitating the efficient capture of A. baumannii onto the surface of the aptasensor. Also, aptamer immobilized on Hemin-graphite oxide (H-GO/Aptamer) was utilized as an electrochemical signal reporter probe by H reduction. This approach improved the detection sensitivity and the aptamer surface density for detecting A. baumannii. Furthermore, under optimized experimental conditions, the aptasensor was demonstrated to be capable of detecting A. baumannii with a linear range of (10 – 1 × 107 Colony-forming unit (CFU)/mL) and a limit of detection (LOD) of 1 CFU/mL (σ = 3). One of the key features of this aptasensor is its ability to distinguish between live and dead bacteria cells, which is very important and critical for clinical applications. In addition, we have successfully detected A. baumannii bacteria in healthy human serum and skim milk powder samples provided using the prepared electrochemical aptasensor.

The functional groups present in the synthetic CQD, rGO-MWCNT, and chitosan facilitate biomolecule immobilization and enhance stability and activity. The fast electron-transfer kinetics and high conductivity of these materials contribute to improved sensitivity and selectivity. Furthermore, The H-GO/Aptamer composite's large surface area increases the number of immobilized secondary aptamers and enables a more stable structure. This large surface area also facilitates more H loading, leading to signal amplification.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The early detection of Acinetobacter baumannii (A. baumannii) bacteria is a crucial process in preventing and controlling infections caused by this pathogen. A. baumannii is a gram-negative bacterium commonly found in healthcare settings, and it is known to cause a wide range of infections, including bloodstream infections, pneumonia and wound infections. Therefore, rapid and reliable diagnosis of A. baumannii is critical for the treatment and control of infectious diseases caused by this pathogen [1–3]. The detection of A. baumannii can be challenging due to its ability to survive on environmental surfaces and its resistance to many antimicrobial agents. Fortunately, several methods are available for the detection of this pathogen. Culture-based methods involve the isolation and identification of A. baumannii from clinical specimens such as blood, sputum or wound swabs. These methods are time-consuming (several days to weeks) and require specialized laboratory equipment and expertise. Molecular methods, such as polymerase chain reaction (PCR) [4], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [5] can detect A. baumannii in clinical specimens with high sensitivity and specificity [6]. Although these technologies offer significant benefits, often entail complex procedures and necessitate professional equipment and personnel, posing challenges for field analysis [7–9]. Therefore, investigators from various disciplines have been concerned with the prevention, detection and resistance of pathogenic bacteria. Recently, efforts have focused on developing effective, speedy, portable, and affordable methods based on affinity reagents such as antibacterial peptides [10], bacteriophages [11], and aptamers [3,12,13].

Biological sensors based on aptamers are emerging as a highly promising technology for molecular detection. Their long-term stability and cost-effectiveness make them an attractive option [14,15]. These biosensors have been widely used for the identification of various species, including proteins, molecules, cancer cells, bacteria and other pathogens [15–22]. Among various detection techniques, electrochemical methods have attracted considerable attention. These methods provide easy solutions for manufacturing, low-cost, high-precision, high sensitivity and cost-effectiveness [23–31]. In recent studies, researchers have investigated the use of conducting polymers and nanomaterial composites to improve the immobilization of aptamers on transducer surfaces, increase signal amplification and enhance sensitivity in the development of electrochemical aptasensors [32,33]. The combination of aptamers with nanomaterials has proven to be a successful approach in developing novel electrochemical aptasensors [15,33,34]. Among nanocomposites, graphene-based ones have high electron mobility, high specific surface area, high mechanical strength, low manufacturing cost and functional component synergy, making them ideal for enhancing sensor sensitivity [35–37]. Multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) are another class of nanoparticles suitable for electrochemical sensor modification due to their conductivity and surface-to-volume ratios, facilitating aptamer immobilization [3,15].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have emerged as a promising nanomaterials with properties ideal for the development of sensitive, stable, and cost-effective aptasensors [38]. The small size, chemical stability, hydrophobicity, easy modification, good conductivity, biocompatibility, and potential to enhance the immobilization of biological molecules such as aptamer chains or DNA make them excellent candidates [33,38,39]. Also, film-forming polymers like chitosan (CS) are valuable in biosensor development, offering exceptional adhesion, film-forming capability and biocompatibility make it an ideal matrix for immobilization of biomolecules [40,41]. However, the non-conductive nature of CS limits its potential for use in electrochemical biosensors. To overcome this limitation, researchers have combined CS with highly conductive nanomaterials, resulting in a substrate material that is suitable for the fabrication of electrochemical biosensors [32,40].

This paper is focused on the role of hemin (H) as an electrochemical signal generating molecule. H is a widely used as an active center of the H-protein family due to its stability and affordability. However, its direct application as a catalyst has been hindered by its limited solubility in water, leading to dimer aggregation and passivation. To overcome this challenge, researchers have explored the immobilization of H on graphene through aromatic π–π interaction [29,42,43]. This approach has shown promising results, as it improves electron transfer, enhances electrostatic interaction and provides a suitable microenvironment to prevent dimerization [44,45].

In this study, we aimed to develop a highly sensitive, electrochemical aptasensor based on rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD for highly sensitive detection of A. baumannii bacteria using a dual signal amplification strategy involving Hemin-graphite oxide (H-GO/Aptamer), which relies on the dual signal amplification. First, the carbon screen-printed electrode (CSPE) surface was modified with rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD to significantly increase the effective surface area of the electrode and for further aptamer immobilization at the surface. The aptamer was used as a capture probe for the recognition of A. baumannii on the surface of rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/CSPE. Also, aptamer immobilized on the H-GO/Aptamer was utilized as an electrochemical signal probe, which increased the aptamer surface density and led to enhancement of the detection sensitivity. Consequently, differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) with H reduction as a redox indicator was used to translate the interaction between aptamer and bacteria. In the presence of A. baumannii, the H-GO/Aptamer interacted with specific epitopes of the bacteria and formed a unique 3D structure containing the bacterium-aptamer complex. In addition, the developed aptasensor was successfully able to monitor A. baumannii in healthy human blood serum and skim milk powder samples and provided satisfactory results. The use of this aptasensor could potentially aid in the early detection and diagnosis of A. baumannii infections. Overall, the design and development of an ultrasensitive electrochemical aptasensor for the detection of A. baumannii is a promising approach to improve disease diagnosis and management. Further research and optimization of these aptasensors could lead to their widespread use in clinical settings.

Multi-walled carbon nanotubes, (MWCNTs, Nanostartech Co., Tehran, Iran). Graphite as a source of graphene oxide (GO), glyoxal (Gly), Chitosan (CS), mercaptoethanol (MCE), Hemin (H), nitric acid (HNO3), skim milk powder, and acetic acid, were of analytical reagent grade and were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich company. A. baumannii (ATCC19606), Escherichia coli (ATTC25922), Bacillus subtilis (PTCC1023), Salmonella typhimurium (ATTC 14028), and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (ATCC15442) strains were kindly provided by the Microbiology Department, University of Mazandaran, Iran. The sequence of A. baumannii aptamer was purchased from Bioneer Company (South Korea). This aptamer with the following sequence was used after dilution with a similar manner as defined in our previous article [3,46].
5′–NH2–ACA GCA CCA CAG ACC ACA TAT CAC ATG CTG TCG CCT TGC GAT ATC AAT TCC AGT GAT GTT TGT CTT CCT GCC-3'.

In this study, electrochemical measurements were conducted using an AUTOLAB PGSTAT30 instrument from Eco Chemie in the Netherlands. Carbon screen-printed electrodes (CSPEs; DRP-C110) were utilized, which were purchased from Metrohm Dropsens. The investigations included cyclic voltammetry (CV) and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) tests in 0.1 M KCl, with 0.01 M [Fe (CN)6]3-/4- (1:1) as a redox couple. For the EIS tests, a frequency ranges of 100 kHz to 0.05 Hz and open circuit potential (OCP) of 0.23 V were used. DPV signals were recorded with a modulation amplitude of 49.95 mV, a time interval of 0.5 s, pulse width of 0.05 s and a step potential of 10.54 mV/s.

The composition analysis and the morphological study were carried out using the Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR; TENSOR BRUKER 27 spectrometer, Germany), X-Ray diffraction measurements (XRD; Philips PW1730 with CuKα radiation, Poland) and Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, MIRA3 - TESCAN company in the Czech Republic). Also, optical density at 600 nm (OD600) for estimation of the cultured bacteria was obtained with a Chrom Tech spectrophotometer (CT-2200, Australia) using a 1.0 cm glass cell.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GO and rGO nanomaterials
ce:section.ce:para: In order to synthesize graphene oxide (GO), the modified Hummers' method was applied [33,47,48]. The process began with the addition of 1.0 g of graphite powder to 40 mL of H2SO4 at 0 °C, and then 5.0 g of KMnO4 was slowly added into the solution under stirring at a fixed temperature of 20 °C. The solution was warmed to 35 °C and stirred for 2 h, before being diluted with 80 mL of distilled water. After cooling the solution with an ice bath, 400 mL of water and 7 mL of 30 % hydrogen peroxide were added to remove any remaining MnO2, resulting in a light-yellow color. To further purify the solution, metal ions were removed by filtering and washing the product with 300 mL of HCl/H2O (1:10 V/V). The acid was then removed by washing the product with 1.0 L of water until a neutral pH was achieved. Finally, the product was dried in an oven at 30 °C for 20 h. The dried synthesized GO powder was applied for preparation of reduced graphene oxide (rGO). To this end, 50 mg of GO was mixed with 50 mL of water to prepare GO suspension. NaBH4 (5:1 w/w) was then added to the suspension at 0 °C and the mixture was stirred for 10 h to ensure complete reduction of GO. The resulting rGO was washed with deionized water (DI-water), collected via centrifugation and dried at room temperature for 24 h [33,49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of amino-functionalized CQDs from chitosan
ce:section.ce:para: A one-step synthesis method was used for the production of amino-functionalized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) with high fluorescence [50]. First, adding 18 mL of 2 % acetic acid to 2.0 g of chitosan, which is then placed in an autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 16 h. After cooling the autoclave, a dark brown product was observed. This mixture was centrifuged to separate the solid particles and deposit for 20 min with 12000 rpm. The upper light brown solution was filtered by filter membrane (0.22 μm) to remove the possible coarse particles and dried at room temperature [33].

To prepare the rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQDs nanocomposite, multi-walled carbon nanotubes (MWCNTs) were activated by reflux in 50 mL of HNO3 (5.00 M) for 24 h. The resulting product, containing –COOH groups was washed with DI-water and then dried at room temperature. Subsequently, a dispersion of 0.05 g MWCNTs and 0.05 g rGO in a 2 % acetic acid solution containing 0.50 M NaCl and 0.1 g CS was prepared by ultrasonication for 3 h. In the next step, 0.05 g of synthesized CQDs were added to the dispersed rGO-MWCNT/CS solution and the resulting suspension was sonicated for 3 h. This led to the formation of a homogeneous suspension of rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQDs nanocomposite [33], as depicted in Scheme 1 A.

First, to prepare H-GO, 0.5 mg of GO and 0.5 mg of H were mixed in 15 mL of DI-water and dispersed under ultrasonication for 30 min. Then, the resulting mixture was stirred for 24 h to obtain H-GO [51]. In the next step, to activate the carboxylic groups in H-GO, 200 μL of the mixture containing EDC/NHS in the ratio (4:5) was added to 200 μL of H-GO dispersed solution for 2 h at 25 °C. Subsequently, 20 μL of 2.0 μM aptamer was added to the mixture and incubated for 60 min. To remove any excess aptamer, the mixture was centrifuged at 9000 rpm for 8 min and washed twice with sterile Phosphate-buffered solution (PBS, 0.1 M and pH = 7.4). Finally, the mixture was washed and stored in 200 μL PBS buffer (0.1 M and pH = 7.4) at 4 °C before use [15], as depicted in Scheme 1B.

All bacteria were placed in sterilized erlenmeyer flasks containing Luria-Bertani broth (5.0 mg/mL yeast extract, 10.0 mg/mL tryptone, and 10.0 mg/mL NaCl, pH 7.0) for 24 h at 37 °C in the incubator for cultivation [3,52]. Next, the cultured bacteria were transferred into a centrifuge tube and centrifuged at a speed of 6000 rpm for 8 min. After separating the upper solution, the remaining sediment containing the grown bacteria was washed by phosphate buffer (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) and centrifuged. This process was repeated three times. Then, the optical density of the cultured bacteria was recorded as a measure of the bacterial population at a wavelength of 600 nm (OD600), finally a suitable population with a specific concentration of bacteria was prepared. In order to prevent contamination of the desired bacteria by aerobic bacteria, all stages of cultivation, growth and washing of bacteria were performed in sterile conditions and next to the flame.

Compared to bacteria, aptamers are small molecules that can bind to bacteria in a manner similar to antibodies and antigens. The binding mechanism between aptamers and bacteria is analogous to antigenic epitopes [53]. This unique binding mechanism has enabled the development of a novel sandwich-type electrochemical aptasensor for the detection of A. baumannii. In this study, we present the analysis of A. baumannii using an electrochemical aptasensor. As depicted in Scheme 1C, the first prepared rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD was dropped onto the CSPE surface to modify the CSPE (rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/CSPE). Second, the Gly (40 wt %) was covalently joined to the rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/CSPE surface via its reaction with –NH2 groups of CS and CQDs to form Gly/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/CSPE. Third, the aptamer (2.0 μM) and MCE (2.0 mM) were fixed on the Gly/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/CSPE step wise. A. baumannii was captured by aptamer via specific recognition, and H-GO/Aptamer nanoprobe modified electrode (H-GO/Aptamer) was later recognized with the captured A. baumannii. In the electrochemical test, differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) signals of H reduction were recorded to realize the detection of A. baumannii. In each step, in order to remove the unabsorbed compounds, the electrode was washed using PBS with pH = 7.4.

The analysis and characterization of synthesized compounds play an important role in determining their properties and potential applications. In this regard, FE-SEM (Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy) has emerged as a highly effective technique for analyzing surface morphology at the nanometer scale [18,]. In this study, the morphologic features of different modified electrodes and GO, H-GO, H and CQD were investigated using FE-SEM and reported in Fig. 1 . The results revealed that the bare CSPE (A) was successfully modified with GO (B) and rGO (C), resulting in a layered surface with stacking nanosheets. Furthermore, the FE-SEM image of rGO-MWCNT/CSPE showed that MWCNTs were well distributed on the surface of rGO (Fig. 1D). After the addition of CS to rGO-MWCNT, the surface of the electrode becomes compacted (Fig. 1E). By adding CQDs to rGO-MWCNT/CS, the surface structure of the electrode appears porous and compact (Fig. 1F). Fig. 1G and H shows the FE-SEM image of GO (G) and H-GO (H) where the GO sample has a two-dimensional smooth. Also, the H compound can be adsorbed on the surface of GO through the π-π interaction, which leads to the accumulation of more layers on the H-GO (Fig. 1H). In addition, the FE-SEM image of CQD synthesized from CS is shown in Fig. 1I, which shows a homogeneous and almost spherical structure.

The FT-IR method was applied to characterize the exfoliated GO and rGO as shown in Fig. 2 A. Changes in the composition of materials clearly cause changes in the characteristic pattern of absorption bands in their FT-IR spectra [54,55]. Based on FT-IR spectrum, the absorbance peaks of GO are related to stretching of O–H, C–O of carboxyl and carbonyl, C–O–C, and C–O bending were observed at 3431, 1717, 1625, and 1059 cm−1, respectively (curve (a)). Moreover, the absorbance peaks relating to the stretching of CC and C–O were respectively observed at 1519 and 1017 cm−1 in FT-IR spectrum of rGO (curve (b)). On the basis of the FT-IR spectrum of rGO, there are no sharp peaks for oxygen functionalities, which indicates the efficient reduction of GO to rGO. Due to deoxygenation of exfoliated GO, the stretching vibrations of hydroxyl groups (O–H) missed at 3431 cm−1 [33,56]. Fig. 2B, curve (a) shows the MWCNT spectra which the peak at 3447 cm−1 can be ascribed to the –OH stretching vibration and the peak at 1633 cm−1 can be attributed to the CO stretching mode of quinone groups at the end of the carbon nanotube. Also, curve (b) shows COOH-MWCNTs spectra, the characteristic peaks of 1746 and 1256 cm−1 corresponding to stretching vibration of CO carboxylic acid and CC bond, respectively. The absorption peaks at 2931 and 2928 cm−1 correspond to symmetric stretching vibration –CH2 and asymmetric stretching vibration [15,57,58]. The FT-IR technique was used to characterize the synthesized CQDs from CS and detect the surface functional groups. Fig. 2C shows the FT-IR spectrum of CS (curve a) and CQD (curve b) from chitosan. The main characteristic peaks of chitosan appeared at 3465 cm−1 (O–H and N–H stretching vibration), 2887 cm−1 (C–H stretching vibration), 1652 cm−1 (N–H bending), 1629 cm−1 (CN and CC groups), 1383 cm−1 (C–H bending of CH2 benzyl group) and 1082 cm−1 (C–H bending of pyranose ring). About CQD, the O–H and N–H vibration and also the C–H vibration of pyranose ring is decreased because of carbonization process. In fact, these differences are due to the decomposition of the chitosan chain and pyranose ring during the thermal synthesis of CQD. However, the vibration of the N–H group is still strong and shows and confirms the presence of amine groups on the surface of as-prepared CQD [49,59]. The main absorption band peaks assigned to stretching vibrations of amine and hydroxyl groups, CONH2 group, and bending vibrations of NH2 groups were observed at 3465, and 1650 cm−1, respectively. There are also many hydroxyl and amino functional groups on the surface of CQD, which cause them to have high hydrophilic properties [49].

Fig. 2D shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD (patterns of the prepared GO (curve a) and rGO (curve b) from graphite. A strong peak located at 2θ = 11.50° (001 plane of GO) as well as a peak located at 2θ = 23.39° (002 plane of rGO) are observed in rGO and the diffraction peak at 11.50° has disappeared, indicating the complete reduction of GO to rGO. The XRD patterns for GO and rGO are in accordance with previous studies. Finally, all results are representative of successful synthesis of exfoliated GO and rGO from natural graphite [33,60]. Fig. 2E shows the XRD patterns of the prepared GO (curve a) and H-GO (curve b). As can be seen in (a), a strong diffraction pattern at 2θ = 11.50° can be attributed as the (001) diffraction plane, which indicates the formation of graphite oxide structure. Also, curve (b) shows two diffraction patterns around 0.24° and 31.5°, which can be assigned to the Fe3O4 magnetic cubic crystal phase [59]. It also has a broad background between 10° and 45°, which indicates that H and GO are well combined in the H-GO multistructure [59]. Fig. 2F shows the XRD patterns of the prepared CS (a) and CQD (b). The strong diffraction pattern at 2θ = 20.1° can be attributed as the (001) diffraction plane, which can be indicating the crystal structure of CS (Curve a). The XRD pattern of CQD shows a broad diffraction pattern located at 2θ = 19.96° (compared to CS XRD pattern, decreases), indicating the amorphous and disordered nature of carbon atoms (Curve b) [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of parameters in preparation of aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: In this research, the experimental parameters involved in the preparation of suggested electrochemical aptasensor were investigated to achieve the best analytical performance. The optimization process includes determining the ideal concentration of the immobilized aptamers on the rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/CSPE and H-GO nanoprobe, aptamers immobilization time, MCE concentration, MCE immobilization time and incubation time of A. baumannii on the rGO-MCE/Apt/MWCNT/CS/CQD/CSPE and H-GO/Aptamer nanoprobe parameters was done, which determined as 3.5 μM, 2.0 μM, 90 min, 60 min, 2.0 mM, 60 min, 90 min and 30 min, respectively. More detailed information on the results of this study can be found in the SM file (Fig. S1- Fig. S8).

In the field of sensor studies, the first and most important step is to fully characterize the sensor platforms at each stage of modification [15,33,61]. Therefore, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and cyclic voltammetry (CV) were used to investigate the electrochemical performance of the fabricated aptasensor in a 0.01 M [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- solution. First, each step was evaluated by measuring the electron transfer resistance (Ret) using EIS. The results in Fig. 3 A showed that the Ret of rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/CSPE (Ret ≈ 28, curve b) was significantly smaller than that of bare SPCE (Re t ≈ 575, curve a), indicating an increase in the effective surface area and conductivity of the modified electrode. Then, as the aptamer was immobilized on the modified electrode, the Ret value increased, indicating successful binding of the aptamer (Ret ≈ 307, curve c). The Ret value increased after the addition of MCE, because the MCE filled the void space of the modified electrode and hindered electron transfer (Ret ≈ 718, curve d) [3]. After the interaction of the immobilized aptamer and A. baumannii, the Ret value increased (Ret ≈ 1208, curve e), indicating the repulsion between the [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- redox probe and the negatively charged A. baumannii [3,62]. Finally, the H-GO/Aptamer nanoprobe was incubated with A. baumannii/MCE/Apt/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/CSPE, which resulted in a decrease in the Ret value due to the large specific surface area and good conductivity of the nanoprobe (Ret ≈ 840, curve f). The obtained experimental data can be effectively modeled using the equivalent circuit shown in the inset of Fig. 3A. This circuit includes several components, namely solution resistance (Rs), the Warburg impedance (W), the charge transfer resistance (Rct) and the constant phase element (GSPE) [3]. Also, each step for fabrication of this aptasensor was studied using CV technique (Fig. 3B), which the CV results are consistent with the EIS. Therefore, the electrochemical aptasensor is successfully fabricated.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Feasibility of aptasensor for detection of A. baumannii
ce:section.ce:para: To demonstrate the feasibility of detecting A. baumannii using a novel sensing platform, we measured differential pulse voltammetry (DPV) signals under various conditions in PBS buffer (0.1 M and pH = 7.4) (Fig. 4 ). The obtained results showed that in the absence of target aptamers and A. baumannii, there is no increase in the DPV signal response (curve a). This indicates that our sensing platform does not generate false positives. Furthermore, negligible peak currents were observed when only the target aptamers (curve b) or A. baumannii (curve c) were introduced with MCE/rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD/CSPE. This highlights the importance of both components for successful diagnosis. However, when the target aptamers and A. baumannii were applied together, we observed a significant peak current that corresponded to H reduction (curve d). This demonstrates the effectiveness of our sensing platform in detecting A. baumannii.

In this study, an aptasensor was successfully fabricated and evaluated for its analytical performance in detection and measurement of A. baumannii. The DPV signal of H reduction was measured at different concentrations of A. baumannii in PBS buffer (0.1 M and pH = 7.4), as shown in Fig. 5 A. It was observed that the intensity current of H reduction increased with increasing concentration of A. baumannii indicating a good linear relationship between the intensity current of H reduction and the logarithm of A. baumannii concentrations in the range of 10 to 1 × 107 CFU/mL (Fig. 5B, n = 3). The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated to be 1 CFU/mL, which calculated with LOD = 3Sd/m, (Sd is the standard deviation of the blank signals (n = 8), and m is the slope of the analytical curve in the linear region) [7,63,64].

The performance of the aptasensor was compared to several previously published articles and the results demonstrated that the designed aptasensor has a higher sensitivity and a more comprehensive linear range than other reports, as shown in Table S1. The proposed aptasensor offers several significant innovations and advantages. The rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD nanocomposite is particularly attractive due to its suitability as a platform for aptamer immobilization. This is attributed to the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups in synthetic CQD, rGO-MWCNT and chitosan in the nanocomposite. Moreover, the presence of MWCNTs and rGO in the nanocomposite exhibits fast electron-transfer kinetics and high conductivity, making it an excellent candidate for electrochemical sensing applications. On the other hand, the H-GO/Aptamer composite provides a large surface area, which enables an increase in the number of immobilized secondary aptamers and the formation of a more stable structure. This large surface area also facilitates more H loading, which ultimately leads to signal amplification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Reproducibility, stability and selectivity of the aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: Reproducibility (interday variations), stability and selectivity are important factors in the development of aptasensors for bacterial detection. In this study, we investigated these parameters for an electrochemical aptasensor designed for the detection of A. baumannii. To assess the reproducibility of the aptasensor, four sensors were prepared under identical conditions for detection of 1.0 × 105 CFU/mL of A. baumannii (Fig. S9). The electrochemical responses of these sensors were found to be highly reproducible with a relative standard deviation (RSD) of 2.05 % (n = 5). We also evaluated the long-term stability of the aptasensor by storing them at 4 °C for 28 days. The results showed that the aptasensor retained 90 % of its initial current response, indicating excellent long-term stability (Fig. S10). This finding is important because it shows that electrochemical aptasensors can maintain their sensitivity over long periods.

To investigate the selectivity of the proposed sandwich type electrochemical aptasensor, in addition to A. baumannii, we cultured and evaluated four other pathogens including Salmonella typhimurium (S. typhimurium), Escherichia coli (E. coli), Bacillus subtilis (B. subtilis), and Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa). As seen in Fig. 6 A, the anodic peak current of H reduction in the DPV signal obtained for these pathogens was much lower than it obtained for A. baumannii (Fig. 6A and B (n = 3)), indicating that the electrochemical aptasensor has excellent specificity for A. baumannii detection.

In this study, we evaluated the effectiveness of aptasensor in the detection of live and dead bacterial cells of A. baumannii at room temperature (25 °C) and under heat (80 °C) in the presence of 105 CFU/mL. The results obtained through CV, EIS and DPV techniques (Fig. S 11), demonstrate that the aptasensor is able to detect differences in membrane integrity between live (curve a) and dead (curve b) A. baumannii cells. In particular, changes in current intensity, ΔEp and Rct values in the presence of [Fe(CN)6]3-/4- probes are clearly observable. These changes are attributed to the presence or absence of specific epitopes on the surface of dead bacterial cells damaged under heat stress (curve b). Overall, our study highlights the potential of aptasensor in detecting and discriminating between live and dead bacterial cells under different conditions, which can be have significant applications in various fields such as food safety, environmental monitoring and clinical diagnosis.

The feasibility of the fabricated aptasensor for practical applications was evaluated by analyzing healthy human serum diluted 1:10 and skim milk powder diluted 1:10 spiked samples with different concentrations of A. baumannii (103, 104 and 105 CFU/mL). As shown in Table S2, the recovery rates ranged from 96.65 % to 112.33 %, with RSDs ranging from 2.40% to 4.35 %. These results indicate that the aptasensor has high accuracy and precision for the determination of A. baumannii in real samples.

On the other hand, we used colony plate counting as a reference method to validate the aptasensor response. This involved spreading a sample with a known concentration of A. baumannii on an LB agar plate and incubating it for 24 h at 37 °C [65]. The resulting colonies were counted and the average of three repetitions was calculated. Then, the relative error of the aptasensor response was compared to the reference method. For example, for a diluted sample with a concentration of 100 CFU/mL, Colony plate counting was performed and the average of three times repetitions was calculated to be 114 CFU/mL. The results showed a relative error of 12.28 % between two methods. This finding has significant implications for the accuracy and reliability of the constructed aptasensor in practical applications.

The present study used an electrochemical aptasensor that employs H-GO/Aptamer as a signal amplifier and rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD as a sensing surface modifier to detect A. baumannii. The functional groups such as hydroxyl (-OH) and carboxyl (-COOH) in the synthetic CQD, rGO-MWCNT, and chitosan increased the immobilized aptamer density, making it a reliable capture probe. Additionally, the fast electron-transfer kinetics and high conductivity of rGO-MWCNT/CS/CQD, attributed to the presence of MWCNT and rGO, further enhanced the sensor's performance. Finally, the high surface-to-volume ratio of rGO-MWCNT and CQDs provides a large area for biomolecule immobilization, and makes it possible to achieve high levels of sensitivity and selectivity. As shown by our results, the electroactivity of H-GO/Aptamer has been successfully used to amplify the electrochemical signal in the proposed aptasensor. Our fabricated electrochemical aptasensor showed a sensitive and selective response against A. baumannii with an acceptable linear range from 10 to 1 × 107 CFU/mL and LOD of 1 CFU/mL. Our fabricated electrochemical aptasensor showed a sensitive and selective response against A. baumannii with an acceptable linear range from 10 to 1 × 107 CFU/mL and LOD of 1 CFU/mL. One of the key features of this aptasensor is its ability to distinguish between live and dead bacteria cells, which is critical for clinical applications. In addition, we have successfully detected A. baumannii bacteria in healthy human serum and skim milk powder using the prepared electrochemical aptasensor. This shows that the fabricated sensor has remarkable stability, high sensitivity and acceptable reproducibility. In conclusion, the developed aptasensor provides a highly promising device for future diagnosis applications.
